subject: Statistics
title: The Power of Question Wording
description: A quick, practical guide to The Power of Question Wording.

hook.text: 'Should the government forbid hate speech?' gets 45% yes. 'Should the government allow hate speech?' gets 62% no.

buildup.text: The exact words in a question change the answer. 'Forbid' feels authoritarian. 'Not allow' feels reasonable.

discovery.text: Survey researchers call this framing effect. The same issue yields opposite results with different wording.

twist.text: Politicians exploit this constantly. Polls can be engineered to produce any desired headline.

climax.text: Good survey design uses neutral wording, pre-tests questions, and randomizes answer order.

punchline.text: Change the question, change the answer.

quiz.question: What is the framing effect in surveys?
options: Different wording of the same question produces different answers
Respondents always agree with the first option
